one_line_takeaway: A hidden, well-engineered user prompt can be largely reverse-engineered from an LLM's answer alone via a BERT-based type/role/context classifier plus ChatGPT-driven reconstruction, showing prompts are not secret (0.833 type accuracy, up to 0.832 prompt similarity), while the paper's own defenses only trade utility for modest protection.
key_finding: Hidden prompts are substantially recoverable from answers alone. The primary type classifier reaches 0.833 accuracy on RetrievalQA/ChatGPT and generalizes to the unseen Alpaca-GPT4 distribution (0.811) and to LLaMA. Hierarchical prediction beats the flat baseline dramatically: overall parameter-extraction accuracy is 0.693 vs 0.234 for the 20-class baseline (RetrievalQA/ChatGPT). Reconstruction achieves 0.832 PS / 0.768 AS for direct prompts and 0.803 PS / 0.703 AS for role-based prompts; the parameter information improves role-based AS from 0.616 (naive baseline) to 0.703. Role assignment matters: the role sub-classifier scores 0.732 when roles are semantically related but collapses to 0.087 when roles are random, implying random roles barely affect the answer. In-context prompts are the weak spot (PS only 0.501) because randomly selected context cannot be recovered from the answer, though AS stays usable. Both defenses reduce attack similarity (e.g., prompt-based drops AS 0.768->0.583) but only at a real utility cost, which the authors concede is a 'subpar trade-off.'
core_contribution: The paper proposes what it claims is the first prompt stealing attack against LLMs that reconstructs a user's original (well-engineered) prompt from only the LLM's generated answer. The attack has two hierarchical modules: (1) a 'parameter extractor' that infers structural properties of the hidden prompt, and (2) a 'prompt reconstructor' that regenerates a similar prompt. The parameter extractor rests on the paper's own observation (from a 10-user questionnaire) that most prompts fall into three types: direct, role-based, and in-context. It uses a BERT-based 3-class 'primary classifier' to predict the type, then two sub-classifiers: a 15-class classifier to predict which role a role-based prompt impersonates, and a classifier to predict how many context examples an in-context prompt uses. The reconstructor prompts ChatGPT ('What question are you asked if you can generate the following answer?') to produce a naive reversed prompt, then assembles the final prompt by appending the predicted role ('Assume you are a [role]') or generating the predicted number of in-context examples. The authors distinguish this from prior work: earlier prompt-stealing targeted text-to-image models (ClipCap/Mokady et al. [22], Shen et al. [35]), and Zhang et al. [53] reversed LLM SYSTEM prompts; this paper is the first to steal user prompts from generated answers.
threat_model: Adversary observes only the answer an LLM generated for a victim's hidden prompt and knows which LLM produced it (justified because answers shared online are usually labeled with their model). The adversary has NO access to the original prompt, no access to model gradients, and cannot fine-tune the target LLM. Goal: (a) infer exact parameter information (prompt type, role, context count) and (b) produce a reversed prompt semantically/structurally similar to the original that elicits a similar answer. Motivation framed around companies selling high-quality prompts (prompt-engineering IP) and downstream use for fake-text detection and jailbreak generation. A separate defender model is also considered: a normal user who can perturb their own prompt or answer but cannot modify the deployed LLM.
limitations: Prompt taxonomy and role list come from a tiny 10-user questionnaire, so the three-category assumption may not cover real prompt diversity. Only ~600 prompts used (cost-limited). In-context reconstruction is fundamentally limited: exact context is unrecoverable (PS 0.501), and LLaMA's short input limit meant in-context prompts were not tested on LLaMA at all, reducing its primary classifier to a 2-class task (making its 0.884 accuracy not directly comparable to ChatGPT's 3-class 0.833). Only two target models (ChatGPT/GPT-3.5, LLaMA) and two QA datasets tested. Success is measured purely by embedding cosine similarity (PS/AS), a soft proxy rather than exact-prompt recovery, and AS reuses the target LLM's own reconstruction ability. Proposed defenses are slow (extra LLM calls per query) and achieve only a weak utility/security trade-off; the authors explicitly call for better defenses.

datasets_used: RetrievalQA (QA prompts; 500 used for training, rest for test)
Alpaca-GPT4 (QA prompts; used as held-out/cross-distribution test set)
~600 total prompts sampled across the two datasets due to query cost
Author-built role-based and in-context prompt sets expanded from a direct-prompt set; 15 roles from a 10-user questionnaire
quantitative_results: Overall hierarchical parameter extractor 0.693 accuracy vs 0.234 for flat 20-class baseline (RetrievalQA/ChatGPT)
Reconstruction role-based prompt: 0.803 PS / 0.703 AS (ChatGPT/RetrievalQA); parameter info raises AS from 0.616 (naive) to 0.703
Reconstruction in-context prompt: 0.501 PS / 0.523 AS (ChatGPT/RetrievalQA) - weakest case
Related vs random role sub-classifier: 0.732 (related) vs 0.087 (random) on ChatGPT/RetrievalQA
Primary classifier scales with training size: 0.507 acc at 100 prompts -> 0.833 at 500 (RetrievalQA/ChatGPT)

Defense (RetrievalQA/ChatGPT, AS 0.768 baseline): prompt-based -> 0.583 AS (utility 0.723); answer-based conspicuous -> 0.486 AS (utility 0.813); answer-based inconspicuous -> 0.691 AS (utility 0.980)
baselines_compared: Flat 20-class classifier (single model predicting role/context directly) vs hierarchical parameter extractor
Naive reconstructor using only the generated answer (equivalent to the direct-prompt case) vs reconstruction aided by parameter-extractor output
Random-guess baselines reported per task in Table 2 (0.333 for 3-class type, 0.067 for 15-class role, 0.250 for 4-class context, 0.500 for LLaMA 2-class)
